title: Answers to Week 3 Hands On Exercise 2 Linear Regression Python INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 – AI for Business Analytics

Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 – Python Linear Regression to predict ‘Total_Spend’ In this activity, we will build a Linear Regression Model using Python to predict the target variable ‘Total spend’, which is continuous.

Load the dependencies First, we need to load the necessary libraries. These libraries help manipulate data, split data into training and testing sets, build models, and assess the accuracy of the performance of the models.

• The pandas library, which is used for data manipulation and analysis. • The train_test_split function to split the data into training and testing sets. • The LogisticRegression class, which is a linear model for binary classification • The accuracy_score function which is used to measure/assess the performance of a model.

import pandas as pd import numpy as np from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error from sklearn import preprocessing import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Load the data Next, we need to load our dataset. To do so, you can download the dataset from Moodle as a CSV file called ‘data_online_retail_customers.csv’ and upload it to your Colab notebook. Let's load the dataset from the csv file provided as follows: # Load the data data = pd.read_csv("data_online_retail_customers.csv")

The code data = pd.read_csv("data_online_retail_customers.csv") reads the data from CSV file 'data_online_retail_customers.csv' and l stores it in a variable called data using the read_csv function from the pandas library (aliased as pd). Clean the data In this step we need to check if there are missing values. # Check for missing values missing_values = data.isna() CRICOS Provider Code 00098G

print(missing_values.any().any())

This dataset does not include any missing values. This is demonstrated by False in the result of the code. In case the above code produces True, this means that the dataset includes missing values and you need address that using the following code. # Remove rows with missing values data = data.dropna()

Check Data Types We need to check the data types of all variables in our dataset. # Print the data type of all variables print(data.dtypes)

You can notice that some columns, like Gender and Promotion_Response, are of type ‘object’ (text). We need to convert text data into categorical variable.

Data transformation In this step, we need to conduct data transformation to convert text data (shown as type object) into numeric or categorical data. e.g. Gender and Promotion_Response should be categorical. You can do that as follows:

# Convert categorical columns to 'category' dtype data['Gender'] = data['Gender'].astype('category') data['Promotion_Response'] = data['Promotion_Response'].astype('category')

Now that text data is transformed into categorical data, you can have a look at the data type of all variables in the dataset to check that the transformation has been applied. Notice that Gender and Promotion_Response are now categorical variables.

# Print the data type of all variables print(data.dtypes)

You would also need to convert categorical variables to dummy variables for use in the logistic regression late.

# Convert categorical variables to dummy variables data = pd.get_dummies(data, drop_first=True)

Now that categorical data is transformed into dummy variables, you can have a look at the data type of all variables in the dataset to check that the transformation has been applied.

# Print the data type of all variables print(data.dtypes)

Feature selection

Linear regression is a fundamental algorithm in machine learning that helps us understand the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables.

In a linear regression model, the primary goal is to predict a continuous target variable (in this case, ‘Total_Spend’) based on one or more predictor variables. Predictor variables should have a meaningful relationship with the target variable to make accurate predictions.

We start by selecting the variables that could influence the target variable ‘Total_Spend’. In this case, Customer_ID, being a unique identifier, cannot serve as a predictor for the target variable ‘Total_Spend’. It lacks any information about customer purchase behaviour, preferences, or spending patterns that could impact their total spending. Instead, we should use variables such as purchase history, loyalty program membership, and other relevant features as predictors in a linear regression model for 'Total_Spend'. These variables are more likely to have a significant and consistent relationship with customer spending patterns.

All other variables can be considered as inputs.

Please refer to the feature selection activity available on Moodle (under Week 4 section) for a step by step guide to a more comprehensive feature selection process.

We need to split our data into input (X) variables (features) and output (y) variables (target).

# Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) X = data.drop(columns=['Customer_ID', 'Total_Spend']) y = data["Total_Spend"]

Preprocess the data (scaling)

To ensure our model performs well, we scale the numeric data. Scaling helps in normalising the data.

# Scale the numeric input data # Identify numeric columns numeric_columns = X.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns

# Scale numeric columns scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X[numeric_columns]) X[numeric_columns] = scaler.transform(X[numeric_columns])

Split data into training and testing sets

We split our data into training and testing sets to evaluate the performance of the linear regression model.

# Split the data into training and testing sets X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

Note: After you split the data into training and testing datasets, you conduct feature selection to select the most important variables for your model. For details please refer to the step-by-step Feature selection in Python and R.

Train a linear regression model on the training data

Next, we create a linear regression model and train it using the training dataset.

# Train a linear regression model on the training data model = LinearRegression() model.fit(X_train, y_train)

Make predictions on the testing data

Once the model is trained, we make predictions on the testing data.

# Make predictions predictions = model.predict(X_test)

Evaluating the model

To understand how well our model performs, we calculate evaluation metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and R-squared.

# Calculate Mean Absolute Error (MAE) mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, predictions)

# Calculate Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, predictions))

# Calculate R-squared r_squared = model.score(X_test, y_test)

# Print evaluation metrics print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE): {mae}") print(f"Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): {rmse}") print(f"R-squared: {r_squared}")

Plotting Actual vs Predicted Values

Finally, we visualise the actual vs. predicted values to see how well our model's predictions match the actual data.

# Plot actual vs predicted values plt.scatter(y_test, predictions) plt.xlabel("Actual Total_Spend") plt.ylabel("Predicted Total_Spend") plt.title("Actual vs Predicted Total_Spend") plt.plot([y_test.min(), y_test.max()], [y_test.min(), y_test.max()], 'k--', lw=2) plt.show()

This process ensures that our model is trained, tested, and evaluated properly, providing us with insights into its performance and accuracy.

Interpreting the Performance of the Linear Regression Model

Let's take a look at the results of the linear regression model, as illustrated in the above scatter plot of Actual vs. Predicted Total Spend.

In the scatter plot, each point represents a pair of actual totals (on the x-axis) and predicted total spend (on the y-axis). The black dashed line represents the line of perfect prediction, where the predicted values exactly match the actual values.

• Spread of Points: Most points are spread around the middle, and the vertical spread indicates the prediction error. The wider the spread from the dashed line, the larger the prediction error. • Underestimation and Overestimation: Points below the dashed line indicate underestimation by the model, while points above the line indicate overestimation.

Mean Absolute Error (MAE): 2402.978 The MAE measures the average magnitude of errors in our predictions, without considering their direction. In this case, our model's predictions are off by an average of about 2403 units of total spend. Lower MAE values indicate better performance model.

Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE): 2775.023 The RMSE is similar to MAE but provides a higher weight to larger errors. It is useful for understanding how the errors are distributed. An RMSE of approximately 2775 indicate, on average, the difference between our predicted and actual total spends which is around 2775 units. Lower RMSE values indicate a better fit.

R-squared: 0.0503 The R-squared value represents the proportion of the variance in the dependent variable that is predictable from the independent variables. An R-squared of 0.0503 means that only about 5% of the variance in the total spend is explained by our model. This is relatively low, indicating that our model does not explain much of the variation in the data.

The findings of the evaluation metrics and the scatter plot, demonstrate that our model has significant errors in its predictions and does not explain much of the variance in total spend. This suggests that our linear regression model may not be the best fit for this data, and we might need to explore more complex models or additional features to improve its performance.

By understanding these metrics and visual analysis, we can better assess the effectiveness of our model and take steps to refine and improve it.

How to interpret the feature coefficients in Linear Regression Model

Now, let’s discuss the coefficients of the features used in our linear regression model. The coefficients indicate how each feature impacts the target variable ‘total spend’.

Display feature coefficients

coefficients = pd.DataFrame({ 'Feature': X.columns, 'Coefficient': model.coef_ }) print(coefficients)

Discussion of the results: Feature coefficients

The Python code above generates a DataFrame containing the feature names and their corresponding coefficients from the linear regression model. The higher the absolute value of a coefficient, the more influential is the corresponding feature. Interpreting the coefficients of a linear regression model involves understanding how changes in the predictor variables affect the outcome.

As you can see, the table displays positive coefficients and negative coefficients. These coefficients provide valuable insights into how each feature affects the total spend. Positive coefficients indicate that an increase in the feature value leads to an increase in total spend, while negative coefficients suggest the opposite. Some results, like churned customers spending more or higher income correlating with lower spend, might need further investigation to understand the underlying reasons.

Interpreting the coefficients of a linear regression model involves understanding how each feature (or independent variable) influences the target variable (in this case, ‘Total_Spend’).

Positive Coefficients Indicate that as the predictor variable increases, the target variable increases.

• Years_as_Customer (317.894133) For every additional year a customer has been with us, their total spend increases by approximately 318 units. Loyal customers tend to spend more over time. • Num_of_Purchases (206.462477) Each additional purchase made by a customer increases their total spend by about 206 units. More frequent purchasers contribute more to the total spend. • Num_of_Returns (127.924740) Each additional return made by a customer is associated with an increase in their total spend by about 128 units. Customers who return items may still be spending more overall. • Email_Opt_In (309.896974) Customers who opt-in for emails tend to spend approximately 310 units more than those who do not. Engaging with email marketing positively influences spending. • Churned (365.699834) Churned customers tend to spend about 366 units more than non-churned customers. This might seem counterintuitive and require further investigation to understand the reasons behind this finding.

Negative Coefficients Indicate that as the predictor variable increases, the target variable decreases.

• Age (-103.991980) For each additional year in a customer's age, their total spend decreases by approximately 104 units. This negative coefficient suggests that older customers tend to spend less. Actionable Recommendation: Create marketing campaigns targeted specifically at older customers to increase their engagement and spending. Offer senior discounts or special promotions for older age groups. • Annual_Income (-108.683575) Each additional unit of annual income is associated with a decrease in total spend by about 109 units. This negative relationship might indicate that higher income customers are more conservative in their spending. Actionable Recommendation: Develop premium products or services tailored for higher income customers that emphasise quality and exclusivity. Consider personalised offers to appeal to their specific needs and preferences.

• Years_as_Customer (317.894133) For each additional year a customer has been with the company, their total spend increases by approximately 318 units. Loyal customers tend to spend more over time. Actionable Recommendation: Implement loyalty programs to reward long-term customers. Offer incentives for continued patronage, such as exclusive discounts, early access to new products, or special events.

• Num_of_Purchases (206.462477) Each additional purchase increases the total spend by about 206 units. More frequent purchasers contribute more to the total spend. Actionable Recommendation: Encourage frequent purchases through loyalty points, subscription services, or bundling offers. Highlight the benefits of repeat purchases in your marketing efforts. • Num_of_Returns (127.924740) Each additional return is associated with an increase in total spend by about 128 units. Customers who return items may still be spending more overall. Actionable Recommendation: While returns are usually seen as negative, this positive coefficient suggests that customers who return items still spend more overall. Ensure a hassle-free return policy to retain these customers and encourage future spending.

• Num_of_Support_Contacts (-316.832092) Each additional support contact decreases the total spend by about 317 units. This might indicate dissatisfaction, leading to lower spending. Actionable Recommendation: Improve customer support to reduce the need for frequent contacts. Invest in training for support staff, enhance self-service options, and proactively address common issues to increase customer satisfaction and spending. • Satisfaction_Score (-21.963414) A higher satisfaction scores slightly decrease the total spend by approximately 22 units. This small negative coefficient suggests that very satisfied customers might not always be the highest spenders. Actionable Recommendation: Although the impact is small, consider conducting deeper analysis to identify why higher satisfaction scores correlate with slightly lower spending. Address any underlying issues that might be causing this trend. • Last_Purchase_Days_Ago (-66.952838) For each additional day since the last purchase (The more days since the last purchase, the lower the total spend), the total spend decreases by about 67 units. More recent purchasers tend to spend more. Actionable Recommendation: Implement reengagement campaigns for customers who haven’t made a purchase recently. Send personalised reminders, special offers, or new product announcements to bring them back. • Gender_Male (-248.301898) Male customers tend to spend about 248 units less than female customers. This negative coefficient indicates a gender-based difference in spending behaviour. Actionable Recommendation: Develop targeted marketing strategies to engage male customers more effectively. Consider product lines, advertising channels, and messaging that resonate better with male audiences. • Promotion_Response_Responded (-429.722692) Responding to promotions correlates with a decrease in total spend by approximately 430 units. This counterintuitive result might require further investigation into the nature of the promotions. Actionable Recommendation: Evaluate the effectiveness of current promotional strategies. Ensure promotions are adding value and not just leading to temporary boosts in sales. Consider personalised promotions that cater to individual customer preferences. • Promotion_Response_Unsubscribed (-238.466543) Unsubscribing from promotions correlates with a decrease in total spend by about 238 units. Customers who unsubscribe from promotional communications tend to spend less. Actionable Recommendation: Investigate why customers are unsubscribing from promotions. Improve the relevance and quality of promotional content to retain subscribers and encourage spending. Offer a preference centre where customers can customise the type and frequency of promotions they receive.

Key Takeaways:

Features with positive coefficients (Years_as_Customer, Num_of_Purchases, Num_of_Returns, Email_Opt_In, Churned) indicate that an increase in these features leads to an increase in total spend.

Features with negative coefficients (Age, Annual_Income, Num_of_Support_Contacts, Satisfaction_Score, Last_Purchase_Days_Ago, Gender_Male, Promotion_Response_Responded,

Promotion_Response_Unsubscribed) indicate that an increase in these features leads to a decrease in total spend.

These coefficients help understand the relationships between different customer characteristics and their spending behaviour. By interpreting these coefficients, businesses can identify key factors that influence spending and adjust their strategies accordingly.
